channel.title: Pitchfork — Album Reviews
channel.description: Pitchfork album reviews, reformatted as Artist - Album

item.title: Happy Today
item.description:
Happy Today

The guitarist and his fellow improvisers demonstrate mind-meld rapport and roof-raising intensity on a pair of side-long cuts recorded at L.A.’s Lodge Room last summer.…

Read full review on Pitchfork →

item.title: American Stories
item.description:
American Stories

Blending Persian and American folk instrumentation, the alt-pop songwriter and producer expands his signature sound on a sharply focused third solo album.…

Read full review on Pitchfork →

item.title: F.I.G
item.description:
F.I.G

With elegant, modern takes on 1980s soft ballads and sophistipop, the former Disney Channel actor’s debut LP is as stylish as it is easygoing.…

Read full review on Pitchfork →

item.title: Body of Work
item.description:
Body of Work

Conceptual mastermind or serial con man? On his latest album of shaky rapping and abstract interludes, the multi-hyphenate creator finds a strange middle ground.…

Read full review on Pitchfork →

item.title: Easy EP
item.description:
Easy EP

The Norwegian duo’s latest EP, inspired by the final track of its 2025 album, forgoes straightforward plots for stranger, smudgier snapshots of life on the precipice of adulthood.…

Read full review on Pitchfork →

item.title: Little Wide Open
item.description:
Little Wide Open

Working with Aaron Dessner, the singer-songwriter balances contentment and mortal anxiety on the most cohesive and tuneful album of his career.…

Read full review on Pitchfork →

item.title: Better Angels
item.description:
Better Angels

On her debut album, the New York singer-songwriter combines bedroom-pop vulnerability and electronic flourishes to dissect the end of a decade-long relationship.…

Read full review on Pitchfork →

item.title: Paradise Metal
item.description:
Paradise Metal

The inspiring and idiosyncratic debut from a 53-year-old priest in Greece pinballs between drone metal and techno Christmas carols, field recordings and hymns.…

Read full review on Pitchfork →

item.title: Lake Mary not mysterious
item.description:
Lake Mary not mysterious

The songwriter’s first album in 11 years transports his suave, smoky style and lounge-lizard character studies to the Florida swamps.…

Read full review on Pitchfork →

item.title: Remember the Humans
item.description:
Remember the Humans

On its first record in nearly nine years, the Toronto indie rock collective uses extended jams and stretches of stillness to highlight the sense of community and connection underpinning its best work.…

Read full review on Pitchfork →

item.title: Lavender Networks
item.description:
Lavender Networks

On her Warp debut, Angel Marcloid offers a dialed-in microcosm of her epic discography, blending black and death metal, scorched-earth electronics, and moments of melodramatic beauty.…

Read full review on Pitchfork →

item.title: Detached From the Rest of You
item.description:
Detached From the Rest of You

The London producer has always taken an unusually subdued approach to club music, but her latest album—a sullen fusion of pop structures and glitchy IDM—is her most fiercely introspective yet.…

Read full review on Pitchfork →

item.title: MR COBRA
item.description:
MR COBRA

To exorcise the memory of a predatory relationship, the experimental artist’s new album and theatrical performance recast the story as a daring avant-pop opera.…

Read full review on Pitchfork →

item.title: BROWN
item.description:
BROWN

Chris Brown’s soulless, hit-chasing new album doesn’t justify his return to the public eye.…

Read full review on Pitchfork →

item.title: Paradise
item.description:
Paradise

The Drain Gang rapper teams with the Swedish production collective for an album whose dreamlike visions of 2010s EDM are tinged with nostalgia and aching melancholy.…

Read full review on Pitchfork →

item.title: Counting Sunsets
item.description:
Counting Sunsets

The ambient-country group’s latest album takes an artfully atmospheric look at the American West, drawing evocative shapes with pedal steel and synth.…

Read full review on Pitchfork →

item.title: Croz Boyce
item.description:
Croz Boyce

The two Animal Collective members team up for a sunny, unhurried record that foregrounds pastoral acoustic guitar and glitchy synths.…

Read full review on Pitchfork →

item.title: Loud Bloom
item.description:
Loud Bloom

In both the Knife and solo, the Swedish producer has honed a uniquely playful style. On his long-awaited debut LP, he turns it toward a radically global vision of pop and dance music.…

Read full review on Pitchfork →

item.title: Dancing on the Wall
item.description:
Dancing on the Wall

The self-professed “queer joy mini-skirt rollerblade” band embraces a darker palette and angstier themes on its fourth album, though pleasure, community, and sex remain lodestars.…

Read full review on Pitchfork →

item.title: Sound Therapy
item.description:
Sound Therapy

The Brooklyn producer’s dance music has always felt unusually introspective; now she adds her own vocals, attempting to coax her fusion of footwork, techno, Jersey club out of the shadows.…

Read full review on Pitchfork →

item.title: Play Monk
item.description:
Play Monk

The jazz quartet interprets the work of Thelonious Monk, expanding classic compositions into absorbing experimental journeys that stretch beyond free jazz.…

Read full review on Pitchfork →

item.title: Caravanserai
item.description:
Caravanserai

Each Sunday, Pitchfork takes an in-depth look at a significant album from the past, and any record not in our archives is eligible. Today, we look back at Santana’s transitional fourth album, a transcendental convergence of rock, psych, and Afro-Cuban styles that absolutely rips.…

Read full review on Pitchfork →

item.title: Super Mario Galaxy / Super Mario Galaxy 2
item.description:
Super Mario Galaxy / Super Mario Galaxy 2

Fusing whimsical electronics and a full symphony orchestra, the Galaxy soundtracks channeled the wide-screen ambition of Hollywood scores, marking a new era for game music.…

Read full review on Pitchfork →

item.title: Train on the Island
item.description:
Train on the Island

On her spellbinding fifth album, Aldous Harding steps closer than ever to the camera lens without coming into focus. It’s an oblique character study of herself, and the best album of her career so far.…

Read full review on Pitchfork →

item.title: sacrificio
item.description:
sacrificio

With witchy atmosphere and guest features by Six Sex, Arca, and Eartheater, the Venezuelan producer’s high-energy experimental club music seeks to defy reggaeton convention.…

Read full review on Pitchfork →

item.title: Limbo
item.description:
Limbo

The Swedish electropop artist’s blissed-out debut confronts body issues with body music, arranging hooks like fridge magnet poetry.…

Read full review on Pitchfork →

item.title: New Racehorse
item.description:
New Racehorse

Led by the magnetic Tom Peter, the prolific lo-fi post-punk band out of Australia adds a human drummer and turns in a 23-minute burst of nonstop fun and creativity.…

Read full review on Pitchfork →

item.title: Midnight Sun – Girls Trip
item.description:
Midnight Sun — Girls Trip

The all-new, all-girls remix album from Swedish pop’s Gen Z ambassador is ready to outmog the original.…

Read full review on Pitchfork →

item.title: Peaches!
item.description:
Peaches!

After a quarter-century as a band, the blues-rock duo offers its second set of covers in just five years. It is weirdly free of feeling.…

Read full review on Pitchfork →

item.title: Cost of Living Adjustment
item.description:
Cost of Living Adjustment

The third album from the Montreal trio breaks free with restless energy and earnest, worldweary songwriting.…

Read full review on Pitchfork →
